author: Macmill-340
text:
NOT ALL VALUES ARE THE SAME KIND
Python keeps track of what type of data each variable holds. The type determines what you can do with it.

INTEGERS (int)
Whole numbers, positive or negative. No quotes.
    age = 25
    score = -10
You can do arithmetic with integers: +, -, *, /

STRINGS (str)
Text. Must be wrapped in single or double quotes.
    name = "Alice"
    city = 'London'
Strings are not numbers — even if they look like one:
    code = "42"    # This is text, not a number

WHY THE TYPE MATTERS
You cannot mix types with +:
    age = 25
    print("Age: " + age)    # TypeError — can't add a string and an integer
    print("Age:", age)       # Correct — use a comma

Adding two strings joins them together:
    print("Hello" + " World")    # Hello World

Adding two integers does arithmetic:
    print(10 + 5)    # 15

You can check what type something is:
    print(type(age))     # <class 'int'>
    print(type(name))    # <class 'str'>

COMMON MISTAKE:
Forgetting quotes around text — Python treats it as a variable name:
    name = Alice     # NameError — Python looks for a variable called Alice
    name = "Alice"   # Correct
common_errors: Forgetting quotes around text (name = Alice causes a NameError). Also: storing a number in quotes when you need to do math with it ('25' instead of 25).
task: Create a variable named 'greeting' with the string value 'Hello', and a variable named 'year' with the integer value 2025.
